questionText: For real numbers $x$, consider the statements $A: x^2 > x$ and $B: x > 1$. Determine which of $\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$, $\Leftrightarrow$ or 'none of these' correctly describes the relationship $A\ ?\ B$, testing each direction and using a counterexample where a direction fails. State your conclusion using the language of necessary and sufficient conditions.

steps.description: Set up the problem and note both directions must be tested.
steps.explanation: The correct connective depends on direction, so we separately ask whether $B$ forces $A$ and whether $A$ forces $B$. Only after both tests can we choose a symbol.

steps.description: Rewrite A as a product inequality.
steps.explanation: Moving everything to one side and factoring turns the inequality into a product compared with zero, which reveals exactly which $x$ make $A$ true.

steps.description: Read off the solution set of A.
steps.explanation: A product of two factors is positive when both are negative or both positive, giving $x < 0$ or $x > 1$. This region is larger than just $x > 1$, which is worth remembering.

steps.description: Begin the forward test: assume B.
steps.explanation: We start from $B$ and try to reach $A$. Knowing $x > 1$ also tells us $x$ is positive, which lets us multiply the inequality safely without flipping it.

steps.description: Multiply the inequality by the positive number x.
steps.workingLatex: x > 1 \Rightarrow x \cdot x > 1 \cdot x \Rightarrow x^2 > x
steps.explanation: Multiplying both sides of $x > 1$ by the positive number $x$ preserves the direction and yields exactly $x^2 > x$. So $B$ genuinely produces $A$.
Every $x$ greater than 1 satisfies $x^2 > x$, so $B$ is enough to guarantee $A$. This makes $B$ a sufficient condition for $A$.

steps.description: Set up the reverse test by seeking a counterexample.
steps.workingLatex: \text{seek } x \text{ with } x^2 > x \text{ but } x \le 1
steps.explanation: To break the reverse implication we want a value satisfying $A$ yet failing $B$. Step 3 showed $A$ also holds for $x < 0$, so that region is the place to look.
A negative value lies in the $x < 0$ part of $A$'s solution set, so it should satisfy $A$ while clearly not exceeding 1.

steps.description: Check the counterexample against both statements.
steps.explanation: Here $A$ is true but $B$ is false, so this single value shows that $A$ does not force $B$.

steps.description: Combine the two tests to pick the symbol.
steps.workingLatex: A \not\Rightarrow B,\ B \Rightarrow A \ \Rightarrow\ A \Leftarrow B
steps.explanation: Only the direction from $B$ to $A$ survives, so the relationship is the single left arrow $A \Leftarrow B$; the counterexample rules out the two-way $\Leftrightarrow$.

steps.description: Translate into necessary and sufficient language.
steps.workingLatex: B \text{ is sufficient, but not necessary, for } A
steps.explanation: Because $x > 1$ guarantees $x^2 > x$, yet $x^2 > x$ can occur without $x > 1$ (as $x = -2$ showed), $B$ is a sufficient but not necessary condition for $A$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $A \Leftarrow B$ only, i.e. $x > 1 \Rightarrow x^2 > x$, but $A \not\Rightarrow B$ since $x = -2$ gives $x^2 = 4 > -2$. So $x > 1$ is sufficient but not necessary for $x^2 > x$; in full, $x^2 > x \Leftrightarrow (x < 0 \ \text{or}\ x > 1)$.
